summary: Graham argues that the decline of the corporate ladder and the rise of startups didn’t suddenly create inequality so much as make existing compensation more visible. Safe, seniority-based jobs had real economic value—akin to hidden wealth—that both statistics and balance sheets ignored. The new model is more liquid and efficient but not as financially different from the old system as it appears.
key_points: The corporate ladder functioned as deferred compensation through job security and future promotions.
Economic statistics and balance sheets ignore the implicit value of safe jobs, skewing perceptions of inequality.
Takeovers in the 1980s undermined the ladder, making long-term climbing less rational.
Startups replace deferred rewards with upfront pay and options, trading stability for liquidity.
The new model increases visible risk but may not be riskier than internal corporate projects.
money_quotes: Economic statistics are misleading because they ignore the value of safe jobs.
A sinecure is, in effect, an annuity.
Why waste your time climbing a ladder that might disappear before you reach the top?
For one thing, it seems a bad plan to treat jobs as rewards.
For better or worse, the idea of the corporate ladder is probably gone for good.
key_insight: The shift from corporate ladders to startups mostly changes the liquidity and visibility of compensation, not the underlying economics of how people were paid for their work.
